passedAssertions.reason: Rust integration test `ship_simple_push_updates_remote_and_clears_ahead` proves simple push succeeds, remote HEAD matches local HEAD, and refreshed status reports ahead=0/behind=0. Frontend handler shows success toast after awaiting push and refreshStatus.
Source inspection shows main-interface disables the push button when `(status?.ahead || 0) === 0`, and mini-view disables when `!status?.ahead`; both enable when ahead > 0 subject to repo readiness.
Source inspection shows main-interface renders `Push (${status.ahead})` and mini-view renders a green numeric count when `status?.ahead` is truthy, matching the ahead value from status.

`ship()` checks behind count and runs `git pull --rebase origin <branch>` before push. Integration test `ship_rebases_then_pushes_when_remote_is_ahead` verifies rebased=true, ahead=0, behind=0, local and remote commits preserved, and remote HEAD matches local HEAD.
Both `main-interface.tsx` and `mini-view.tsx` show `toast.loading("Syncing with remote...")` whenever `status.behind > 0`, and dismiss it on completion or failure.

`ship()` classifies rebase conflicts as `GitError::RebaseConflict`, aborts the rebase, and returns without leaving rebase state. Integration test `ship_conflict_aborts_rebase_and_leaves_repo_clean` verifies conflict text, unchanged local HEAD, clean status, and no rebase state remains. Frontend maps conflict messages to a user-friendly conflict toast.
The same conflict integration test verifies no data loss: local HEAD is unchanged and `shared.txt` still contains the local version after the failed push attempt.

Push/rebase classification no longer routes behind/divergence failures to `AuthFailed`: `classify_rebase_error` returns `RebaseConflict` or `PushRejected`, and frontend conflict handling emits conflict-specific messaging instead of auth language. Cargo tests cover conflict and rejected classification helpers.

Both views set `isPushing` before invoking push, render `Loader2` while pushing, disable the push control during the operation, and reset `isPushing` in `finally`, restoring normal state after success or failure.

Main-interface and mini-view share equivalent smart-push logic: same behind check, same syncing toast text, same conflict and partial-failure toast branches, same backend `send("push")` call, and same `refreshStatus()` on success.

`git_ship` invalidates cached status in Rust after success, and both frontend handlers call `await refreshStatus()` before showing the success toast. Integration tests confirm post-push status resolves to ahead=0.

blockedAssertions.reason: No automated test or existing harness in this repo simulates the specific partial failure where `git pull --rebase` succeeds but the subsequent push fails due to a transient transport/network error. Source inspection shows intended handling via `classify_push_error(stderr, rebased)` and dedicated frontend toast text, but without a controllable Tauri/runtime fixture this user-surface assertion cannot be fully proven.

frictions: The tauri-rust-cli validation surface is guidance-limited to source inspection plus existing automated verification; no real Tauri runtime harness is available here for observing live toasts or inducing post-rebase transport failures.
`cargo test` must be run from `apps/desktop/src-tauri` via `--manifest-path`; running it from the repo root fails because there is no top-level Cargo.toml.
blockers: VAL-PUSH-012 requires a controllable push failure after a successful rebase in a real Tauri/runtime-backed flow; existing tests do not cover that scenario and browser mode cannot exercise git push.
summary: Validated 12 assigned Smart Push assertions for the tauri-rust-cli surface using mission guidance, targeted source inspection, `bun run typecheck`, and `cargo test --manifest-path apps/desktop/src-tauri/Cargo.toml`. Eleven assertions pass based on direct code paths plus Rust integration coverage for simple push, auto-rebase push, conflict abort cleanup, parity across views, and status refresh. One assertion (VAL-PUSH-012) remains blocked because the repository lacks an automated/runtime harness to force a push transport failure after a successful rebase and observe the resulting user-surface behavior end-to-end.
